root.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Decision Assist Guide
root.summary: This 42-page guide introduces Cúram Decision Assist in Cúram 8.1.2. It explains what Decision Assist is, why agencies use it, and how it supports transparent, configurable, rule-based assessments with determination configurations, packages, IEG questionnaires, outcomes, and decision matrices. It covers assessment lifecycle actions such as answering, making, canceling, completing, cloning, comparing decisions over time, plus versioning and localization support. The guide also walks through sample setups for Asthma and Juvenile Detention screening assessments, including case association, questionnaire and matrix creation, release, and linking to assessments. The final pages contain legal notices, privacy policy, and trademarks.

children.title: Note
children.summary: This note directs readers to review the Notices section on page 41 before using the product or its documentation. It also includes copyright information for Merative US L.P. covering 2012 and 2024.

children.title: Edition
Edition — pages 5-14
children.summary: Introductory section for Cúram Decision Assist, describing the module’s purpose and how it supports configurable, transparent assessments based on simple rules. Covers key concepts: decision-making requirements, benefits, assessment lifecycle, and administration setup. Explains determination configurations, stand-alone vs multiple-source assessments, automatic consolidation, and change-of-circumstance support. Also introduces determination packages, IEG questionnaires, typical pictures, presentation data, and outcomes. Useful as the overview and configuration entry point for Decision Assist.

children.title: Edition — pages 15-24
children.summary: This section introduces Cúram Decision Assist configuration and the decision matrix editor. It covers how to configure decision matrices for determination packages, assessment integration, stand-alone vs multiple determinations, version control, and localization. It explains matrix logic (AND/OR, Boolean/enumerated/integer answers), outcomes, scoring, answer priority, contradictions, and how questions from released IEG questionnaires are added. It also details the matrix wizard, editor layout, validation rules, and context-menu actions for questions, answers, scores, priorities, filters, and contradictions/outcomes.

children.title: Edition — pages 25-34
children.summary: This section explains Decision Assist admin functions and assessment lifecycle, including matrix header actions (show related/all questions, copy/insert/delete combinations, set contradiction messages), cloning decision matrices, and how determination-based assessments are created, package-selected, answered, consolidated, and compared. It then walks through a sample Asthma assessment setup: case association, determination configuration, gender-specific packages, questionnaire creation in IEG, decision matrix rules, contradictions, release, and linking to the assessment. The section begins a Juvenile Detention screening assessment overview.

children.title: Edition — pages 35-40
children.summary: This section explains how to configure a Juvenile Detention Decision Assist screening assessment in Cúram. It covers the 22-question questionnaire structure and flow logic, then walks through setup steps: add the assessment code, create the assessment and case association, configure a standalone determination with one package, build a questionnaire with IEG preconditions, create and release a decision matrix with Level 1–5 outcomes, and associate the configuration with the assessment for caseworker use.

Overview of Cúram Decision Assist: what it is, why agencies use it, and how it supports simple, configurable, transparent rule-based assessments. Covers core configuration objects—determination configurations and packages, IEG questionnaires, outcomes, typical pictures, decision matrices, versioning, localization, and assessment lifecycle actions such as answering, making/canceling/completing, cloning, and comparing decisions over time. Includes sample setup walkthroughs for Asthma and Juvenile Detention screening assessments, showing case association, questionnaire and matrix creation, release, and linking to assessments.

children.title: Notices
Privacy policy
children.summary: This section covers legal notices: fictitious sample data disclaimers, licensing terms for sample programs, warranty/liability limits, a link to Merative’s privacy policy, and trademark acknowledgments for Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle, Linux, Microsoft, and UNIX. It is useful for locating copyright, usage rights, and trademark information rather than product functionality.

children.title: Trademarks
children.summary: States that example names in the document are fictitious and may resemble real entities only coincidentally. Notes the licensed program and sample code are provided under the Merative Client Agreement and that sample programs are offered “AS IS” without warranty. Includes the Merative privacy policy link. Lists trademark ownership for Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle/Java, Linux, Microsoft/Windows, and UNIX, and notes other names may be trademarks of others.

This notices section gives legal terms for using the publication: personal and enterprise reproduction limits, preservation of proprietary notices, restrictions on derivatives and redistribution, and no implied rights beyond those stated. It includes export-control compliance, an “AS-IS” no-warranty disclaimer, patent notice, disclaimer for third-party product information and external websites, and Merative copyright ownership. Useful for locating usage permissions, legal restrictions, and warranty/liability terms.

children.title: Contents
children.summary: Table of contents for the Cúram Decision Assist guide. It lists the main sections and page numbers: overview of Decision Assist; configuration topics such as determination settings, packages, questionnaires, outcomes, decision matrices, assessments, version control, and localization; the Decision Matrix and its maintenance/cloning; the lifecycle of determination-based assessments; sample assessments (Asthma and Juvenile Detention screening); and closing notices, privacy policy, and trademarks. Useful for navigating the manual, not substantive content.

children.title: 1 Cúram Decision Assist overview
1.1 Understanding Decision Assist
Requirements of a decision-making solution
children.summary: Explains why agencies need a simpler decision-making model alongside complex rule sets: quick configuration, execution, transparency, consistency, and tracking outcomes over time with reasons for change. Introduces Cúram Decision Assist as the module that dynamically configures and runs assessments using simple, administrative rules. Notes example use cases such as medical, fraud, housing, and child care assessments, including family risk assessments, where Decision Assist provides outcomes and the agency acts on them.

children.title: Usage of Cúram Decision Assist
children.summary: Cúram Decision Assist lets agencies dynamically configure and run assessments using simple, administrative rules instead of complex rule processing. This section explains why a decision-making solution is needed, emphasizing transparency, consistency, quick configuration, and tracking outcomes over time. It maps Decision Assist to those needs and introduces administration and determination-based assessment components. Examples include medical, fraud, housing, child care, and family risk assessments, where the module produces outcomes and the agency decides the next action.

children.title: Cúram Decision Assist benefits
Ability to maintain simple rules
children.summary: Explains how Cúram Decision Assist supports simple rule maintenance: decisions can be made by comparing collected evidence to configured rules without calculations. Contrasts these simple yes/no outcome rules with more complex statistical or legislated rules, and notes that Decision Assist handles both but is especially suited to simple eligibility or status outcomes. Also highlights configurable rules by parameters like age, gender, and program, enabling consistent, efficient decision making and repeatable assessments.

children.title: Ease of configuration
children.summary: This section explains that Decision Assist is easy to configure because rules can vary by parameters such as age, gender, and program without custom development. It notes that assessments can use different evidence requirements based on client characteristics, enabling flexible rule application. The section also frames this as part of broader benefits: supporting simple rules, improving efficiency and consistency, and using configured data to produce uniform decision outcomes.

children.title: Efficiency and consistency in decision making
children.summary: Explains how Cúram Decision Assist improves decision making efficiency and consistency through configurable, rules-based assessments. Covers simple rules versus complex/statistical rules, and how evidence is compared against configured rules to produce outcomes without manual calculations. Describes ease of configuration for different programs, ages, and genders, and highlights a repeatable assessment lifecycle for questionnaires, reviews, and re-decisions. Emphasizes that decisions are driven by centrally defined administration rules, ensuring uniform results across users.

Cúram Decision Assist benefits from easy configuration of rules-based assessments, improving efficiency and consistency in decision making. It supports simple yes/no outcome rules by comparing collected evidence to configured rules without calculations, while also handling more complex statistical or legislated rules. Rules can vary by program, age, and gender without custom development. The section also notes repeatable assessment workflows for questionnaires, reviews, and re-decisions, with uniform outcomes driven by centrally defined administration rules.

children.title: Overview of Decision Assist configuration
children.summary: This section explains how Cúram Decision Assist is configured for program-based client assessments. It covers determination configurations, packages, questionnaires built in IEG, and rules defined in the Decision Matrix. It highlights that answer combinations and outcomes can vary by package characteristics such as age and gender. It also positions Decision Assist as supporting simple rule-based decisions, easy configuration, and consistent assessment outcomes across users.

children.title: Overview of determination-based assessments
children.summary: This section explains determination-based assessments in Cúram Decision Assist. It describes how questionnaires collect evidence, how answers may come from multiple sources and be consolidated, and how a decision matrix produces one or more outcomes. It gives an example of juvenile detention screening and notes support for change-of-circumstance determinations with effective dates. It also introduces configuration of determination settings, including defining required evidence, collection methods, and decision logic, plus the stand-alone indicator.

Explains why agencies need a simpler decision-making model alongside complex rule sets: quick configuration, execution, transparency, consistency, and tracking outcomes over time. Introduces Cúram Decision Assist as a module for dynamically configured assessments using administrative rules, covering use cases like medical, fraud, housing, child care, and family risk assessments. Summarizes benefits such as easy rule maintenance, simple yes/no determinations, configurable rules by program/age/gender, repeatable workflows, and uniform outcomes. Also outlines core configuration elements: determination configurations, packages, IEG questionnaires, and Decision Matrix rules.

children.title: 1.2 Configuring Decision Assist
Configuring determination settings
Configuring stand alone determinations
children.summary: Explains how to configure determination assessment behavior using three indicators: Stand Alone, Multiple Sources, Automatic Consolidation, and Support CoC. Stand Alone restricts an assessment to one determination configuration; determination-based assessments auto-use it, while Decision Assist can allow multiple configs when not stand-alone. Multiple Sources enables third-party evidence collection, and Automatic Consolidation controls whether the system or user selects the answer to use. Support CoC enables repeatable determinations over time when client circumstances change.

children.title: Recording answers from multiple sources and consolidation
children.summary: Explains assessment configuration flags for determination-based assessments: Multiple Sources enables third-party evidence and the Third Party Request function; Automatic Consolidation controls whether the system or user selects the final answer, with the system choosing the latest answer when enabled; Support CoC enables repeatable determinations over changing client circumstances. Also notes the Stand Alone indicator elsewhere on the page, limiting an assessment to a single determination configuration.

children.title: Configuring change of circumstances
children.summary: Explains the Support CoC indicator for configuring change-of-circumstances scenarios in determinations. It enables assessments to be repeatable over time when client circumstances change, allowing new evidence and a new determination to be created for each change. The section contrasts this with cases where the agency prefers to create a new assessment and rerun the determination, in which case Support CoC should not be set. It also notes related indicators above this section: Stand Alone, Multiple Sources, and Automatic Consolidation.

Defines determination configuration settings for Decision Assist: Stand Alone restricts an assessment to one determination configuration and auto-uses it for determination-based assessments; Multiple Sources enables third-party evidence and the Third Party Request function; Automatic Consolidation lets the system or user choose the final answer, with the system using the latest answer when enabled; Support CoC allows repeatable determinations over time when client circumstances change, otherwise a new assessment should be created.

children.title: Configuring determination packages
children.summary: Explains how to configure determination packages in Cúram Decision Assist. Determination packages group questionnaires and decision matrices by program, gender, and age so the system can choose the right questions and rules for an assessment. Also covers configuring questionnaires through Intelligent Evidence Gathering (IEG), including question text, third-party aliases, and linking questionnaires to one or multiple packages. Includes basic answer type/value examples: boolean, code table, and numeric.

children.title: Configuring questionnaires
Configuring typical pictures
children.summary: Explains how to configure typical pictures, presentation data, and outcomes in Cúram Decision Assist. Typical pictures capture expected answers for a prior determination outcome and can be resynchronized if questionnaire questions change, but answer comparison is not supported for determination-based assessments. Presentation data controls whether questionnaires are mandatory, recommended, or not displayed, and can block decisions if mandatory questionnaires are unanswered. Outcomes define determination outputs, can be shared across decision matrices, and are configured separately then linked where relevant.

children.title: Configuring presentation data
children.summary: This section explains presentation data for determination-based assessments. It defines how questionnaires can be marked mandatory or recommended, so the decision matrix can verify required questionnaires were answered before making a decision. It applies to both standalone and non-standalone determinations; in non-standalone cases, a questionnaire may be set to Do Not Display if the previous determination makes it irrelevant. The section also briefly introduces outcomes as reusable decision outputs configured separately from decision matrices.

Explains how Decision Assist questionnaires are created and managed through IEG. Covers configuring question text and answer data types in the IEG editor, including aliasing for multiple source configurations so different users may see different wording. Notes that questionnaires can be reused across multiple determination packages or tied to a specific program/client characteristic. Also lists supported answer types and values, including boolean, code table, and numeric ranges.

children.title: Configuring outcomes
children.summary: This section explains how to configure determination outcomes, which are the outputs of a decision such as benefit level, abuse level, or fraud risk. It notes that outcomes are defined separately from decision matrices so they can be reused across multiple matrices. Example: a common outcome like “High” can be created once and linked to both risk and support assessments.

children.title: Configuring decision matrices
children.summary: This section explains how to configure decision matrices in Cúram Decision Assist. It defines decision logic for assessment outcomes within a determination package (product, gender, age), notes that multiple matrices can exist for different factor types, and describes the matrix structure of questions, answers, outcomes, scores, and answer combinations. It also gives a high-level overview of outcome/scoring modes, especially cumulative scoring and multiple outcomes, with examples of mapping score ranges to risk levels and returning several support-related outcomes.

children.title: Configuring Decision Assist assessments
Setting up determination configurations for an assessment configuration
children.summary: This section explains how to link determination configurations to an assessment configuration. It notes that the number of determinations allowed depends on the Stand Alone indicator, and that multiple determinations may be needed for multiple problems or sequential decision steps. However, although the Administration component supports multiple determinations, determination-based assessments do not yet support running multiple determinations at once; only one determination can be run per assessment creation.

This section explains how to configure Decision Assist assessments by linking an assessment configuration to determination configurations. It covers setting the assessment type to Decision Assist, choosing the DA_resolveAssessment home page, and associating the configuration with integrated case, product delivery case, or investigation case types. It also explains when multiple determinations are needed (multiple problems or sequential steps) and notes a key limitation: although Administration supports multiple determinations, determination-based assessments currently run only one determination at a time.

children.title: Version control support
children.summary: This section explains version control for determination-based assessments: assessments use the administration configuration valid on the assessment date, ensuring consistent decisions and supporting effective-from dates. It covers released versus edit versions for determination packages, questionnaires, decision matrices, and typical pictures, plus release dependencies (subcomponents must be released before parent components). It also notes that new versions can be created from previous data, and briefly mentions localization support for questionnaires, translated configuration names, outcomes, and medical conditions.

children.title: Localization support
children.summary: This section explains localization in Cúram Decision Assist. Questionnaires can be translated into multiple languages using the IEG editor, including question pages, groups, and individual questions. It also supports translating determination configuration names, outcomes, and medical conditions. A key benefit is enabling communications, such as letters, in a person’s preferred language.

Configures Decision Assist administration for determination-based assessments: stand-alone determinations, multiple sources/third-party evidence and automatic consolidation, and support for change of circumstance. It explains how determination packages group questionnaires and decision matrices by program/gender/age, how questionnaires are built in IEG with aliases and reusable answer types, how outcomes are defined separately for reuse, and how decision matrices map answers to outcomes and scores. It also covers linking assessments to Decision Assist, supported case types, and the current limitation that only one determination runs at a time.

children.title: 1.3 The Decision Matrix
Using the Decision Matrix
children.summary: Explains that the Decision Matrix is the rule engine used within a determination package to determine outcomes from assessment answers. It is intended for simple rule sets, not complex calculations or data comparisons. Administrators configure assessment rules with AND logic across questions and OR logic within a question, and the matrix matches answer combinations to an outcome. Useful if you need to understand how decision logic is configured and what kinds of rules the matrix can and cannot support.

children.title: Elements of the Decision Matrix
Questions
children.summary: This section explains the “Questions” element of a decision matrix for determination-based assessments. It covers how questions are selected from released IEG questionnaires, added as matrix rows, and limited to those relevant to the decision type. It also describes answer handling for Boolean, enumerated list, and integer inputs, including subrows and multiple answer values. Finally, it introduces score configuration, including cumulative and per-outcome scoring, and notes that scores are optional and depend on matrix setup.

children.title: Answers
children.summary: Explains the “Answers” part of the Decision Matrix for determination-based assessments. Decision Assist supports Boolean, code table/enumerated list, and numeric answers, configured in IEG alongside the question. When questions are added to the matrix, answer-specific subrows are created: integers can use ranges or fixed values, enumerated lists show code table values, and Boolean questions show True/False. Multiple answer values can map to different outcomes. Also covers score generation, including cumulative score and score-per-outcome options.

children.title: Scores
children.summary: Explains how Decision Matrix scoring works in determination-based assessments. A matrix can generate scores depending on configuration, with two scoring modes: cumulative score and score per outcome. If scoring is enabled, a score column appears and each answer value can be assigned a score. The section also briefly situates scores within matrix elements such as questions and answers, and notes that matrices support Boolean, enumerated list, and integer answers.

children.title: Answer priority
children.summary: Explains how Decision Assist assigns priorities to answers in a decision matrix so the most favorable or highest-priority answer can be identified when multiple sources exist. Also introduces related matrix concepts: outcomes, two outcome-determination methods (answer matching vs score comparison), contradictions, and decision matrix maintenance. Includes how matrix header settings such as version, type, outcomes, and scoring options control later matrix setup.

children.title: Outcomes
children.summary: This section explains how Decision Assist defines and uses outcomes in decision matrices. Outcomes are agency-defined program decisions, such as risk levels or benefit rates, and can be determined either by matching assessment answer combinations or by comparing assessment scores to outcome score ranges. It also covers answer priorities, contradiction handling in matrices, and the distinction between decision matrix header settings and matrix data, including versioning, outcome type, and scoring options.

children.title: Contradictions
children.summary: Explains how Decision Assist handles contradictory answers in a determination-based assessment. Organizations can define specific answer combinations as contradictions in the decision matrix’s contradiction column, and configure a message to return when such a combination is encountered. This section sits within the broader discussion of decision matrix configuration, alongside answer priority, outcomes, and matrix maintenance.

Explains the decision matrix’s core elements for determination-based assessments: questions, answers, and scores. Questions from released IEG questionnaires are added as rows, limited to those relevant to the decision type. Answers can be Boolean, code table/enumerated list, or numeric, with subrows for ranges, fixed values, or True/False, and multiple answer values can map to different outcomes. Scores are optional and configurable as cumulative or per-outcome, adding a score column when enabled.

children.title: Maintaining decision matrices
Decision Matrix header
children.summary: This section explains the decision matrix header in Decision Assist. It covers header-level settings such as matrix version, matrix type, whether multiple outcomes are required, and scoring options. It also notes that these header choices control how the matrix data must be configured, are carried forward to later matrix versions, and can be modified. Creating the header enables launching the matrix editor.

children.title: Creating a new Decision Matrix
children.summary: Explains how to create a new Decision Matrix using the creation wizard when a matrix header exists but no data has been added. The wizard has four setup steps: selecting questionnaires, selecting questions from those questionnaires, selecting available outcomes, and adding extra options such as contradictions, priorities, and scores. Also introduces the Decision Matrix editor as the user interface for creating, editing, and validating matrices, and notes its main layout components: main menu, filter matrix, validation panel, and main matrix content.

children.title: Decision Matrix editor
Decision Matrix editor layout
children.summary: This section introduces the Decision Matrix editor and its layout. It explains that when a matrix header exists without data, a creation wizard can be launched with four setup steps: Questionnaires, Questions, Outcomes, and Option. It also describes the editor as a user-friendly environment for creating, editing, and validating decision matrices, and identifies its main components: main menu, filter matrix, validation panel, and main matrix content.

children.title: Main menu
children.summary: Describes the Decision Matrix editor’s main menu panel and its high-level actions. The buttons are Save, which validates and saves the matrix and surfaces any errors in the validation panel; Validate, which checks the matrix configuration for errors and lists them in the validation panel; and Export, which outputs the matrix as a CSV file for formatting and printing.

children.title: Filter matrix
children.summary: Describes the matrix filter control and its context menu for hiding/showing matrix columns. Includes “Show All” to toggle all outcomes and contradictions, a separate “Contradictions” toggle, and per-outcome checkboxes to hide individual outcome columns. Notes this is a usability feature for navigating large matrices.

children.title: Validation panel
children.summary: Describes the matrix validation panel in the Cúram editor. Validation can be triggered manually or on save to catch configuration errors such as duplicate rules. Any validation errors are shown in a panel located below the menu buttons and above the matrix content. When errors are present, the panel lists them, and hovering over an error highlights the corresponding offending sub-columns.

children.title: Main matrix content
Questions
children.summary: Describes the Questions column in the Cúram Decision Assist main matrix and its context menu. Covers actions to add answers to numeric questions (default Value, optional Min/Max), notes that boolean questions already have true/false answers and cannot add more, and codetable questions start with all possible answers and can be restored via Restore All Answers. Also explains Delete Question, which removes the selected question and all its answers from the matrix.

children.title: Answers
children.summary: This section describes the Answers table in Cúram 8.1.2. It explains context menu actions for answer rows, including switching numeric answers between specific value and range (Use Value, Use MinMax) and deleting answers when permitted. It also covers editing and clearing Scores and Priorities via header context menus. Finally, it explains Contradictions/Outcome checkboxes and the “Show Related Questions” action, which filters questions to display the logic for selected contradiction/outcome combinations.

children.title: Scores
children.summary: This section explains the Scores column in the Answers table. It says score text boxes let users assign a score to each answer, and the Scores column header has a context menu with a single action: Delete Scores, which removes scores from all answers. It also continues with adjacent columns: Priorities and Contradictions/Outcomes, including a header action to show related questions.

children.title: Priorities
children.summary: This section describes the Answers table controls for questions, focusing on the Priorities column. Users can assign priority values to each answer via text boxes, and the Priorities header context menu offers Delete Priorities to clear all priorities. It sits alongside related controls for Scores and Contradictions/Outcome, including context menu actions for deleting scores and filtering related questions.

children.title: Contradictions and outcomes
children.summary: This section documents the Contradictions and outcomes area in Cúram Decision Assist, focusing on its checkbox matrix and related context menus. It explains actions to show related/all questions, add or delete contradiction/outcome columns, and manage individual combination headers with copy, insert before/after, and delete. It also notes that contradiction combinations can have localized explanatory messages set via an Edit Messages pop-up, and that these messages are shown to users when a decision is made.

Main matrix content describes the decision matrix’s core columns and context-menu actions. It covers Questions (add numeric answers, restore codetable answers, delete questions), Answers (switch numeric values/ranges, delete answers), Scores and Priorities (enter or clear values via header menus), and Contradictions/Outcomes (select combinations, show related/all questions, add/delete combinations and columns, copy/insert/delete headers, and set localized contradiction messages). It also notes validation behavior and constraints such as duplicate numeric answers and conflicting priorities.

Describes the Decision Matrix editor and how to create, edit, validate, and export matrices. It covers the creation wizard for new headers (Questionnaires, Questions, Outcomes, Option), the editor layout, main menu actions (Save, Validate, Export), outcome/filter controls, and validation panel behavior. Also explains matrix content operations for questions, answers, scores, priorities, and contradiction/outcome combinations, including context-menu actions, copy/insert/delete, related-question filtering, localized contradiction messages, and validation constraints.

Explains how decision matrices are maintained in Decision Assist, splitting configuration into matrix header and matrix data. The header defines version, type, multiple-outcome and scoring options, which drive later matrix setup. It then covers the creation wizard for a new matrix and the editor layout and actions: save, validate, export, filters, and validation panel behavior. Also details matrix content operations for questions, answers, scores, priorities, contradictions, outcomes, combination copying/insertion/deletion, and localized contradiction messages.

children.title: Cloning decision matrices
children.summary: Decision matrices can be cloned within the same or a different determination package to avoid rebuilding similar logic from scratch, then modified for differences such as age-range rules. The section also introduces determination-based assessment lifecycle basics: creating assessments for integrated case, product delivery case, or investigation case; selecting a case member as the subject; and using stand-alone determination configurations. It explains that a determination package contains questionnaires and decision logic, and the system chooses the best match based on client product, age, and gender criteria.

Introduces decision matrices in determination packages as the rule engine for simple outcome logic, not complex calculations. Covers supported answer types (Boolean, code table, numeric), question selection from released IEG questionnaires, scores, priorities, contradictions, and outcome matching by answer combinations or score ranges. Also explains matrix maintenance in Decision Assist: header vs data, creation wizard, editor layout, validation, filtering, export, and editing actions like add/delete/copy/insert. Useful for understanding how decision logic is configured and maintained.

children.title: 1.4 Determination-based assessment lifecycle
Creating a determination-based assessment
Selecting the determination package
children.summary: This section explains how determination packages are selected during a determination-based assessment. A package contains the questionnaires and decision logic, and the system chooses the best match using configured criteria such as product, age, and gender. It describes retrieving case and participant information, finding all effective-date packages, and selecting the package that best matches the client. It also notes that assessments may use multiple packages when rules differ by age, gender, or agency program.

This section explains how to create a determination-based assessment and how the system selects the appropriate determination package. It covers eligible case types (integrated case, product delivery case, investigation case), selecting a case member as the assessment subject, setting the effective date, and creating a single stand-alone determination. It also describes determination packages as containers for questionnaires and decision logic, and how the system matches package criteria such as product, age, gender, and agency program to the client and assessment date.

children.title: Answering questionnaires
Configuring questionnaires
children.summary: This section explains how questionnaires are configured in Decision Assist using Intelligent Evidence Gathering (IEG). It covers creating and maintaining question scripts, defining question text and answer data types, and using third-party aliases for different respondent sources. It also notes that questionnaires can be shared across multiple determination packages or tied to a specific package when program or client characteristics require it.

children.title: Recording third-party answers
children.summary: Explains how to record third-party answers in Cúram when a determination collects evidence from multiple sources. Users can select questionnaire questions for third-party opinion, including unanswered questions and those needing a second opinion, via question aliases in IEG. A third-party request is created with status Not Sent, changes to Sent when sent, but no automatic communication is generated. When responses arrive, users record answers, and multiple third-party answers can be stored for one question.

children.title: Consolidating answers
Recording third-party answers
children.summary: Explains how Cúram records third-party questionnaire answers in multi-source determinations. Users can select unanswered questions or those needing second opinion, create a third-party request, and send it manually; the request status changes from Not Sent to Sent, but no automatic communication is generated. Responses can be recorded from multiple third parties for the same question. The section also notes that question aliases in IEG support multi-source evidence collection, and that answers may later need consolidation before decisions are made.

Explains how Cúram consolidates questionnaire answers from multiple sources before decision making. Third-party requests can be created for unanswered questions or those needing second opinion; requests move from Not Sent to Sent, but no automatic communication is generated. Multiple third-party responses can be recorded for the same question. Consolidation may be automatic (latest answers replace earlier ones) or manual, and determination configuration controls this. Questionnaires must be fully consolidated before a decision is made, with status tracked as No, Partially, or Fully.

This section explains how questionnaires support decision-based assessments in Cúram Decision Assist. It covers questionnaire creation and maintenance in IEG, including question text, answer data types, and third-party aliases for different respondent sources. It also describes collecting and recording third-party answers, with requests moving from Not Sent to Sent but without automatic communication. Finally, it explains consolidation of multiple answers—automatic or manual—and the requirement that all questionnaires be fully consolidated before a decision is made.

children.title: Making decisions
children.summary: This section explains how Decision Assist makes determinations after evidence collection by executing a decision matrix, matching questionnaire answers to configured rules to return outcomes and scores, or contradiction reasons when answers conflict. It covers repeating decisions, superseding prior ones, and the limit once a determination is completed. It also describes canceling assessments (including cancellation of all decisions), completing assessments for downstream processing, and cloning assessments for periodic reviews or change-over-time scenarios.

children.title: Canceling determination-based assessments
children.summary: Determination-based assessments can be canceled when created in error or when an error is introduced. A completed assessment cannot be canceled because further processing, such as benefit award, may already depend on it. Canceling an assessment also cancels all associated decisions. The section also notes that assessments can be marked complete in Decision Assist only after at least one decision exists, and completed assessments cannot be updated.

children.title: Completing determination-based assessments
children.summary: This section explains completing determination-based assessments in Decision Assist. It notes that assessments can be marked complete after at least one decision exists, indicating the decision is final and usable for downstream processing such as benefit payments. Completed assessments cannot be updated, though new determinations can later be made through change-of-circumstance processing. It also contrasts completion with cancellation, which is only possible before completion and cancels all decisions, and introduces cloning for periodic review assessments.

children.title: Cloning determination-based assessments
children.summary: This section explains assessment cloning for periodic reviews in Decision Assist. It says decisions can be repeated and superseded until a determination is completed, after which evidence changes require change-of-circumstance processing if supported. It also notes canceled assessments cancel all decisions, and completed assessments cannot be updated. Cloning lets a new determination-based assessment reuse a previous one as the base for a later review, making it easier to update only changed client evidence for recurring benefit assessments.

children.title: Changing and comparing decisions over time
children.summary: This section explains that determination-based assessments can be made multiple times and compared over time, including across different determinations for the same client. It notes that decisions may change due to new evidence or changing circumstances, and comparison can include decision outcomes, scores, questions, and answers. The section also mentions using this capability to monitor client progress and risk, such as abuse or neglect, and to support corrective actions based on trends.

Covers the determination-based assessment lifecycle in Decision Assist: creating assessments for integrated case, product delivery, or investigation cases; selecting the best-matching determination package by product, age, gender, and program; gathering evidence through IEG questionnaires and third-party answers; consolidating multiple answers; making and repeating decisions via a decision matrix; and handling completion, cancellation, and cloning for periodic reviews/change over time. Also notes completed assessments are final for downstream processing and cannot be updated.

children.title: 1.5 Configuring sample assessments
Asthma assessment overview
children.summary: Overview of a sample Asthma assessment in Decision Assist Administration. It explains the assessment objective: collect information about a client’s asthma to determine severity for possible use in disability eligibility decisions. The sample uses a single determination type and is a standalone assessment, unlike older multi-determination medical assessment designs. Outcomes can be Mild, Moderate, or Severe, based on answer combinations; in some cases no outcome is produced, leading to an alternate result type.

children.title: Configuring the Asthma assessment
Configuring the assessment
children.summary: This section explains how to configure the Asthma Decision Assist assessment. It notes the assessment must be added to the Assessment Name code table as an application assessment (code AST-01, description “Asthma Assessment,” selectable), then published. It also outlines the overall setup steps for a new assessment: link it to a case type, create a determination configuration, add determination packages, create questionnaires, and build rules in the Decision Matrix.

children.title: Creating the determination configuration and determination packages
children.summary: Explains how to create a Decision Assist determination configuration for asthma eligibility and how to set up gender-based determination packages. The configuration is named “Asthma Adult,” type Medical, with Multiple Sources, Standalone, and Support CoC Processing enabled, while Automatic Consolidation is not used. Because a peak flow question differs by sex, two packages are created: “Asthma Female” and “Asthma Male,” selected at runtime by claimant gender. Also notes creating the assessment and associating it with the Assistance integrated case type.

children.title: Configuring questionnaires
children.summary: This section explains how to configure Decision Assist questionnaires and decision matrices for an asthma assessment. It describes creating one shared IEG questionnaire (“Asthma Adult,” type Medical) used by both determination packages, then releasing it for matrix use. It also covers setting up separate male/female decision matrices with no scoring or multiple outcomes, adding contradiction combinations, ordering outcomes by priority (Severe, Moderate, Mild), and adding question/answer logic in the matrix editor.

children.title: Configuring decision matrices
children.summary: This section explains how to configure questionnaires and decision matrices for the asthma assessment in Cúram Decision Assist. It describes creating one shared questionnaire (Asthma Adult, Medical) as a single IEG script, then adding it to both determination packages. It also outlines setting up two decision matrices (female and male variants), with no multiple outcomes or scoring, but with contradiction combinations. Outcomes are prioritized Severe, then Moderate, then Mild, and matrix rules are built by adding question answers and combinations.

children.title: Associating the determination configuration with the assessment
children.summary: This section explains how to link a released determination configuration to an assessment so it can be used in the Determination Based Assessment component. It instructs the user to open the Assessments tab, expand the Asthma Assessment, select New Determination Configuration, and add the Asthma Adult configuration. It concludes that the Asthma assessment is then ready for use.

Explains how to configure the Asthma Decision Assist assessment. It must first be added as an application assessment in the Assessment Name code table (AST-01, “Asthma Assessment”), then published, created in Decision Assist, and linked to the Assistance integrated case type. It also describes creating the “Asthma Adult” Medical determination configuration with Multiple Sources, Standalone, and Support CoC Processing enabled, plus gender-specific Female and Male determination packages. Finally, it sets up one shared Asthma Adult questionnaire and separate female/male decision matrices with contradiction handling and outcome priority rules.

children.title: Juvenile Detention screening assessment overview
children.summary: Overview of a sample Juvenile Detention screening assessment configured in Decision Assist Administration. It explains the assessment objective (determine youth placement level), the five possible outcomes (Level 1 Secure Detention through Level 5 Release), and the questionnaire structure: 22 questions across 7 categories. It also notes that question groups are conditionally asked based on earlier answers and gives an example rule from the flowchart for Level 2 placement, with a note that some logic can be handled via IEG preconditions.

children.title: Configuring the Juvenile Detention screening assessment
Configuring the assessment
children.summary: This section explains how to configure a new Decision Assist assessment for Juvenile Detention screening. It covers the required five setup steps, including adding the assessment name to the Assessment Name code table (code JUVDET), creating the assessment in Decision Assist, and associating it with the Assistance integrated case. It also introduces the next stage: creating the determination configuration and determination packages, noting this is a standalone, point-in-time screening assessment with no change-of-circumstances processing.

children.title: Creating the determination configuration and determination packages
children.summary: This section explains how to configure a Juvenile Detention screening assessment in Decision Assist. It covers the setup steps, including adding the assessment name to the AssessmentName code table, creating the Decision Assist assessment, linking it to the Assistance integrated case, and then creating the determination configuration and determination packages. Key facts: the assessment is standalone, has a single “Screening” determination type, and does not allow change-of-circumstances processing.

children.title: Configuring questionnaires
children.summary: Explains how to configure questionnaires in Decision Assist for a juvenile detention screening example. Advises using a single questionnaire with 7 question groups in one IEG script, though separate questionnaires are possible. Describes creating the questionnaire from the determination package, editing questions in the IEG Editor with preconditions based on the question flow, then releasing it so it can be used in a Decision Matrix. Also notes all questions are mandatory on IEG script creation.

children.title: Configuring the Decision Matrix
children.summary: Describes how to configure the Juvenile Detention decision matrix in Decision Assist. A single Screening determination configuration and one package are used, with no multiple sources or automatic consolidation. A single questionnaire is released and added to one decision matrix with no multiple outcomes or scoring. Outcomes Level 1 through Level 5 are added in priority order, then rules are entered using Boolean question combinations and AND/OR logic. The section also condenses the assessment logic based on IEG preconditions.

children.title: Associating the determination configuration with the assessment
children.summary: Explains how to associate a determination configuration with an assessment so it can be used by the Determination Based Assessment component. Steps shown: open the Juvenile Detention screening assessment from Decision Assist shortcuts, choose New Determination Configuration, and add the Juvenile Detention configuration. Concludes that the Juvenile Detention assessment is ready for caseworker use. Pages 41–42 are Merative notices, licensing, privacy, and trademark information.

Explains how to configure a Juvenile Detention screening assessment in Decision Assist. Covers the five setup steps: add assessment name JUVDET to the AssessmentName code table, create the assessment and link it to the Assistance integrated case, create a standalone Screening determination configuration with one package, build a questionnaire (single IEG script or split questionnaires), configure a Decision Matrix with Level 1–5 outcomes and Boolean rules, and associate the configuration back to the assessment. Includes notes that there is no CoC processing, multiple sources, or scoring.

Section 1.5 explains how to configure two sample Decision Assist assessments: Asthma and Juvenile Detention. It covers assessment setup, linking to the Assistance integrated case, creating determination configurations/packages, building questionnaires, and defining decision matrices/rules. The Asthma example uses one medical determination with gender-specific packages, multiple sources, standalone and CoC processing, and outcomes of Mild/Moderate/Severe plus contradiction/undetermined results. Juvenile Detention uses one screening determination, one package, Boolean question groups with IEG preconditions, and outcomes Level 1–5 placement.

Overview of Cúram Decision Assist, a module for dynamically configured, transparent, consistent assessments using simple administrative rules instead of complex calculations. Explains why agencies use it, its benefits, and core configuration objects: determination configurations, packages, IEG questionnaires, and decision matrices. Covers determination-based assessment flow: collecting client/third-party evidence, consolidating answers, running rules, and supporting change of circumstances. Also introduces configuration settings like stand-alone, multiple sources, automatic consolidation, and CoC, plus sample Asthma and Juvenile Detention assessments.
